title: Collecting & Cleaning Data
concept: Before any analysis, you gather data and clean it — fixing errors, blanks, and duplicates. Garbage in, garbage out.

options.text: Examining data to find patterns and answer questions

exercises.explain: Right — analytics makes sense of data. But first the data has to be usable.

cards.title: Real data is messy
cards.body: Typos, blanks, duplicates, wrong formats. You can't analyze a mess — first you collect it, then you clean it.

cards.title: Collecting data
cards.body: Gathering it from sources: web forms, sensors, databases, APIs, surveys, app logs.

cards.title: Cleaning data
cards.body: Fixing problems: missing values, duplicates, typos ("NYC" vs "New York"), wrong units, and impossible outliers (age = 999).

cards.title: A messy customer list
cards.body: The same person listed twice, an email typo, age recorded as 999, blank phone fields. Clean all that before you count anything.

cards.title: Cleaning is most of the job
cards.body: Analysts spend roughly 80% of their time collecting and cleaning — not the 'fun' analysis. Remember: garbage in, garbage out.

exercises.prompt: Why clean data before analyzing it?

options.text: Errors and duplicates lead to wrong conclusions
It makes the file look prettier

exercises.explain: Dirty data → wrong answers. Cleaning protects every conclusion that follows.
Removing duplicates is classic cleaning. So is fixing typos and filling/flagging blanks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: analysts often spend most of their time collecting and cleaning data.
exercises.explain: True — around 80%. Clean data is the unglamorous foundation of good analysis.
Garbage in, garbage out — bad input data produces bad results.

exercises.prompt: A survey accidentally recorded the same response 5 times because of a bug. If you don't clean it, what happens?

options.text: Your results will be skewed and misleading

exercises.explain: Duplicates over-count that response and bias the whole result. Clean first, conclude second.
